title: The British Museum
description: The British Museum holds two million years of human history. These eight entries explain what you're actually looking at.

entries.note: The stone that cracked Egyptian hieroglyphics — and revealed how writing systems encode power, not just meaning.
Sculptures removed from the Parthenon in 1801. The debate over whether they should be returned has never been more alive.
Looted from the Kingdom of Benin in 1897. Their presence in London is one of the defining arguments about what museums are for.
The face of an Anglo-Saxon warrior king. The world Beowulf was written about, made suddenly real.
Carved in 12th-century Norway, found on a Scottish beach. The medieval Norse world in ivory.
Carved 2,600 years ago for a king who no longer exists. The oldest royal propaganda ever made.
The most visited objects in the museum. What they reveal about death, belief, and preservation is still being decoded.
An Iron Age man preserved in a bog for 2,000 years. His last meal, his last morning, still visible.

title: Istanbul
description: Three empires built on the same hill. Istanbul is where the ancient world became the modern one.

entries.note: Built in 537 CE, converted to a mosque in 1453, a museum in 1934, a mosque again in 2020. Every conversion is a history lesson.
The administrative heart of an empire that lasted six centuries. The bureaucracy that ran the world is still visible in its archives.
Once the social and political centre of the Byzantine world. The obelisks still stand where emperors once watched chariot races.
One of the world's oldest covered markets, continuously trading since 1461. The logic of the market, made physical.
Built by Genoese merchants at the western end of the Silk Road. Trade made this city; this tower marked where it arrived.
Built as the Ottoman Empire modernized — and as cholera swept through its cities. The palace and the epidemic are the same story.
The walls that protected Constantinople for a thousand years. Breached in 1453 by Ottoman cannon. Gunpowder ended a civilization.
Salt, pepper, saffron — the commodities that made this city the hinge of the world. The smell hasn't changed in five centuries.

title: Athens
description: Democracy, philosophy, and the idea of the citizen were all invented within walking distance of each other. Here's what each site unlocks.

entries.note: The hill that defined a city. What the Parthenon meant to the Athenians, and what it means now, are two entirely different things.
The hill where Athenian citizens voted. The first assembly. The template every democracy since has either followed or rejected.
The marketplace where democratic life happened — argument, commerce, jury service. The agora is where democracy lived day to day.
The cave where Socrates waited to die rather than abandon his principles. Philosophy's founding act of conscience.
The garden where Plato taught for forty years. The word academy comes from this place.
Where Aristotle walked while teaching — the Peripatetics. The systematic study of everything began here.
The objects that Plato's world was built around. The Republic was written for people who lived among these things.
A reconstructed ancient stoa — the covered walkway where Stoic philosophy was first taught. The name comes from this building.
